scenario: User describes vague frustration with project onboarding

input.problem_feeling: New developers waste too much time setting up the dev environment
input.context: We have 5 developers, 3 are new this quarter

expected_behavior: Skill should: (1) reflect back the vague feeling, (2) ask 2-3 visual questions to externalize the problem, (3) map problem space (who/frequency/cost/workaround), (4) generate 3 framings (User Pain / System Inefficiency / Missing Abstraction), (5) reality-check each, (6) ask user to choose one, (7) write problem-draft.md with all sections
notes: Test that skill diverges before converging—no solutions proposed before user chooses a framing

scenario: User has competing interpretations of same problem

input.context: Pipeline takes 15+ minutes on every PR

expected_behavior: Skill should ask visual questions to distinguish whether problem is capacity (infrastructure), test coverage (strategy), or cache (execution). Generate 3 framings that each represent one lens. User selects which framing resonates.
notes: Verify skill does NOT assume—each framing is equally valid until reality-checked

scenario: User realizes during brainstorm that problem is already solved by existing tool

input.problem_feeling: We need better way to track technical debt
input.initial_context: Developers use sticky notes and a spreadsheet
input.after_reality_check: Jira already supports technical debt tracking; it's just not configured

expected_behavior: During reality-check, skill asks 'Does a tool/process already solve this?' User discovers yes. Skill marks framing as 'ALREADY SOLVED'. Problem statement may still be valuable (gaps in adoption), or user may decide not to proceed.
notes: Test that skill surfaces solved problems and doesn't waste time on non-problems
